findings.description: touch() reads the session directly without purging or checking expires_at, so it resurrects an already-expired (but not-yet-purged) session by sliding its expiry forward and returning True, contradicting the expiry semantics enforced everywhere else (get/active_count purge first).

user_sessions() neither purges nor filters by expires_at, so it returns expired sessions for a user — inconsistent with get()/active_count() which exclude expired ones, breaking any caller that uses it to count or display active sessions.

create() assigns self._sessions[token] with no collision check, so a duplicated token from _new_token() would silently overwrite and destroy an existing user's session (astronomically unlikely at 62^32, but unguarded).

Out-of-scope (security) noted in passing: _new_token() uses the random module (Mersenne Twister), which is predictable and unsuitable for session tokens; use secrets.token_urlsafe/SystemRandom instead — defer detail to critic-security.
